rulings.Case Title: RICHARD NEILL SAUER VS. MEDICAL BOARD OF CALIFORNIA
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Petition For Writ Of Administrative Mandate
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Friday, August 28, 2020, Line 1, Hearing On Petition For Writ Of Administrative Mandate (Part 1 of 2 for purposes of entry of tentative ruling). Richard Sauer's petition for writ of administrative mandate is denied. Following hearings, respondent California Medical Board placed Dr. Sauer's medical license on probation for five years - to run concurrently with probation from another case. (Dec. p. 17.) Dr. Sauer's writ memorandum argues that the "Board did not proceed in the manner required by law." He complains that, while he was not found incompetent, a Board finding employs "the language of 'incompetence'" by using the phrase "general fitness to practice." However, the Board's decision clearly states that Sauer's conduct was "negligence" (see, e.g., id. at pp. 18-19); no due process violation occurred. So too with Sauer's complaint that he paid the hearing reporter for a transcript. Dr. Sauer's memo also argues that the "Board's findings are unsupported by the weight of the evidence." The parties agree that independent judgment is the standard of review, which is "synonymous with the preponderance of the evidence standard." (Ettinger v. Board of Medical Quality Assurance (1982) 133 Cal.App.3d 853.) As discussed below, I find the Board's findings to be supported by the evidence. Based largely on expert testimony from Dr. Shing Lui, the Board found that Dr. Sauer: -"failed to perform an adequate initial neurological consultation" on the patient by not including in his report, any "past medical history of allergiesalcohol and drug useor vital signs" save blood pressure. (Dec. p. 11; II RT 32-33; Lui Rpt.) Dr. Sauer pits his own testimony against Dr. Lui's. Notably, however, Lui was qualified at hearing to testify as an expert, while Sauer was not. (Sauer failed to timely designate an expert.) -"failed to legibly and accurately document relevant medical information." (Dec. pp. 11-12; II RT 33-34; Lui Rpt.) Dr. Sauer effectively conceded this point. (III RT 13.) (Tentative Ruling continues in Part 2 of 2) =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: RICHARD NEILL SAUER VS. MEDICAL BOARD OF CALIFORNIA
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Petition For Writ Of Administrative Mandate (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Friday, August 28, 2020, Line 1, Hearing On Petition For Writ Of Administrative Mandate (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling). -ordered "the transcranial Doppler test without medical indications." (Dec. p. 12; II RT 35-36, 39-41; Lui Rpt.) Dr. Sauer again pits his personal views against Dr. Lui's expert opinions. -in "the most serious issue," "prescribed Keppra to [the] patient with a history of depression and suicide." (Dec. p. 13: II RT 41-44; Lui Rpt.) Dr. Sauer claims a "difference of medical opinion" - an argument that would have had more force had he actually introduced expert opinions at hearing. -"failed to effectively communicate with" the patient "regarding her treatment." (Dec. pp. 13-14: II RT 44-45; Lui Rpt.) Dr. Sauer labels this "highly subjective;" Dr. Lui disagrees. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the movant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (End of Tentative Ruling) =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: MICHAEL JOHNSON VS. CALIFORNIA DEPARTMENT OF MANAGED HEALTHCARE
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petitioners Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Compelling Further Responses To Petitioners First Set Of Special Interrogatories
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, August 28, 2020, Line 1, PETITIONER MICHAEL JOHNSON's Motion For Order Compelling Further Responses To Petitioners First Set Of Special Interrogatories Pro Tem Judge Bruce Highman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is granted as to all 21 interrogatories. The court notes that the agency has not provided a detailed factual basis in support of its decision to withhold the documents. The court finds the discovery is likely to aid in the resolution of the particular issues presented in the proceeding. The discovery is therefore necessary to resolve whether the agency has a duty to disclose and is justified despite the need for an expeditious resolution of this proceeding. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to bruce.highman@highmanlaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If parties have arranged for a court reporter to appear, please include the following in your email: (1) ensure that they are California Certified Shorthand Reporters; (2) the reporter's full contact information [name, CSR number, personal work email, and phone number]. If the reporter is not a California Certified Shorthand Reporter, the transcript cannot be certified for use in California courts. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must e-mail it to the Judge Pro Tem. =(302/JPT) [LATE]

rulings.Case Title: MACHELE WEBB VS. DSM ENGINEERING PLASTICS, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Machele Webb Motion For New Trial
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Friday, August 28, 2020, Line 5, Plaintiff Machele Webb's Motion For New Trial. Plaintiff Machele Webb's motion for a new trial - the papers claim four grounds - is denied. First, plaintiff argues that the factual findings were not supported by the evidence. To the contrary, the summary judgment order's findings - reviewed again in considering this new trial motion - are supported by the evidence cited. Plaintiff's spin on the facts simply repeats arguments already addressed. Second, plaintiff asserts for the first time that this is a "mixed-motive" case. However, "[n]ew theories that could have been raised, but were not, is not one of the causes that permits a new trial." (Ins. Co. of State of Pa. v. Am. Safety Indemnity Co. (2019) 32 Cal.App.5th 898, 922-23.) Third, it is claimed that discretion was abused by considering evidence from two "interested" witnesses. Rare is the case without interested witnesses. This evidence was credible and consistent with the other evidence. Fourth, plaintiff claims "ambush" by theories in defendants' opening summary judgment papers. However, a key purpose of the 75-day notice period for summary judgment motions is to "provide the responding party with adequate time to conduct discovery that may be necessary to fully respond to the motion." (Lackner v. North (2006) 135 Cal.App.4th 1188, 1208.) As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: JAMES HILTON JR VS. PAGANI WORLDWIDE LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Pagani Worldwide Llc, Francesco Zappacosta, Pagani Automobile America, Inc. Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint (Stipulated Continuance)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Friday, August 28, 2020, Line 6, Defendants Pagani Worldwide LLC, Francesco Zappacosta, and Pagani Automobile America, Inc.'s Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint. Defendant Pagani's demurrer to the first amended complaint is sustained with leave to amend to plead facts showing a timely claim and exhaustion of administrative remedies. Zappacosta's demurrer to causes of action one and three are sustained without leave to amend. Plaintiff can only plead those discharge claims against his employer; they are not cognizable against his manager. (See Jones v. Torrey Pines Partnership (2008) 42 Cal.4th 1158, 1173; Reno v. Baird (1998) 18 Cal.4th 640, 662.) As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Calendar Matter: Second Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Defendant Elina Emily Todorovs Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents, And For Monetary Sanctions Against Defendant And Defendants Counsel
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, August 28, 2020, Line 5, Second Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Defendant Elina Emily Todorovs Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents, And For Monetary Sanctions Against Defendant And Defendants Counsel Pro Tem Judge Bruce Highman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. The Pro Tem Judge will email the full tentative ruling directly to the parties. As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to bruce.highman@highmanlaw.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If parties have arranged for a court reporter to appear, please include the following in your email: (1) ensure that they are California Certified Shorthand Reporters; (2) the reporter's full contact information [name, CSR number, personal work email, and phone number]. If the reporter is not a California Certified Shorthand Reporter, the transcript cannot be certified for use in California courts. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must e-mail it to the Judge Pro Tem. =(302/JPT) [LATE]

rulings.Case Title: SYED HUSAIN VS. ELINA EMILY TODOROV ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Friday, August 28, 2020, Line 8, DEFENDANT ELINA TODOROV'S DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT. Defendant Elina Todorov's demurrers to the first amended complaint (FAC) are overruled. Demurrers 1-5 center on the "anti-heart balm" statute: Civil Code Sec. 43.5, which bars causes of action for "alienation of affection." However, the FAC pleads that defendant and plaintiff were friends for 14 years (dating for five months of that) and that defendant owes for concert tickets, travel expenses, storage, failure to return a key and credit cards and the like. Defendant cites no authority for the notion that Civil Code Sec. 43.5 bars all suits between former friends who ever dated, however briefly. Defendant's other demurrer couples the heart-balm statute with the sham pleading doctrine. This is an effort to saddle plaintiff with his original complaint, which did run afoul of the statute. But the doctrine's purpose "is to prevent [an] amended pleading which is only a sham, when it is apparent that no cause of action can be stated truthfully." (JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A. v. Ward (2019) 33 Cal.App.5th 678, 690.) Here, the FAC re-writes the original complaint to state facially legitimate causes of action rather than "heart balm." As a result of the COVID-19 emergency, all attorneys and parties are required to appear remotely. Hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. To appear at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)
